myths.Agent_1:
Long ago, before the mountains had voices, lived a spirit named Aethel. Aethel was made of wind and sorrow, condemned to wander the craggy peaks of the highest ranges. She possessed a voice so beautiful that the stars would weep silver dew just to hear her sing. But Aethel was terribly lonely. The stones could not listen, and the wind only howled over her melodies.

One twilight, the Sun God, taking pity on her eternal solitude, offered her a gift. "I cannot give you a companion," he bellowed, his voice painting the sky in hues of orange and violet, "but I can ensure you are never unheard again."

He shattered a piece of the twilight sky and embedded it into the canyon walls. From that day forth, whenever Aethel spoke or sang, the canyon walls would catch her voice and throw it back, creating a chorus of her own making. Over time, Aethel faded into the breeze, but her gift remained. Now, whenever a traveler calls out into the deep mountain valleys, the lingering magic of the Sun God awakens, and Aethel’s children—the echoes—answer back, ensuring no voice is ever truly alone. They are a reminder that even in barren lands, someone is listening.
myths.Agent_2:
Long ago, the towering mountains were completely silent. They were the oldest of the earth’s children, carved from solid ancient stone, but they had no voices of their own. They could only quietly listen to the wind howling and the cold rivers rushing. 

Among the air spirits was Aella, a creature of boundless chatter. She loved to sing and tell grand stories. But Aella was lonely. The wind only whispered. She desperately wanted someone who would talk back. 

One day, Aella flew to the highest peak and shouted into the deep canyon, "Is anyone there?" 

The mountain, moved by her deep loneliness, attempted to reply. Having no voice of its own, it could only catch her words in its rocky cradle and gently toss them back. 

"Is anyone there?" the canyon replied, softer this time. 

Aella laughed, absolutely delighted. "I am here!" she cried out. 

"I am here!" the mountain returned. 

From that day on, Aella never left the peaks, and the mountains were never silent again, forever answering the calls of those who speak to them. This is why, when you shout into a valley, the spirit of the stone throws your words back.

myths.Agent_2:
Myth: Before the world had memory, the great stone titan Oros stood in absolute silence. He loved Aella, a wandering spirit of the wind who sang melodies of breathtaking beauty. Yet, Aella was plagued by sorrow, believing her songs vanished into the void, unheard and unappreciated. Oros yearned to comfort her, to tell her that her voice was the only warmth he knew, but his granite throat could form no words.

Desperate, Oros pleaded with the Star Goddess. Moved by the titan’s silent devotion, she plucked a shimmering comet from the night sky and shattered it into a million crystal fragments, scattering them across the deep canyons and towering peaks of Oros’s body. 

The next morning, when Aella wept and cried out, "Is anyone listening?" a miraculous thing occurred. The crystal-lined valleys caught her voice and gently called back, "Listening... listening." 

Aella smiled, her loneliness banished forever. She never left the mountains again, filling them with endless song. Today, when you shout into a rocky canyon, it is the magic of the shattered comet that answers. The mountains are no longer silent; they are forever echoing the love of Oros for the wind.

Myth: In the eldest days of the world, the great peaks were deaf and mute, carved from cold, unfeeling granite. High above them danced Lyra, a spirit woven from mist and starlight. She was a creature of boundless song, casting breathtaking melodies into the void, but she suffered a terrible loneliness. The stars were too far to hear, and the wind simply tore her songs apart.

The Stone King, the oldest mountain, felt Lyra’s tears falling upon his snowy summit like silver dew. Though he possessed no voice, her sorrow moved his ancient heart. Desperate to comfort the weeping spirit, the Stone King begged the Moon for a miracle. The Moon, touched by the mountain's compassion, bathed the deep canyons in a magical luminescence, softening the harsh granite so it could catch and hold sound. 

The next time Lyra cried out, "Hold me," the magic of the Moon and the will of the Stone King caught her plea. From the glowing ravines, a gentle, resonant voice replied, "Hold me." 

Overjoyed, Lyra filled the valleys with endless song. To this day, the mountains are never silent, faithfully returning the words of any lonely traveler who seeks a friend.

myths.Agent_1:
Myth: Before time was recorded, the colossal mountain titan, Kaelen, stood in eternal silence. He harbored a secret love for Zephyr, a wandering spirit born of mist and dawn light. Zephyr possessed a voice of heartbreaking beauty, weaving melodies that painted the skies. Yet, she was profoundly lonely, weeping because her songs simply vanished into the empty air, unacknowledged and unreturned. Kaelen ached to comfort her, to tell her that her voice was the very beating of his stone heart, but he had no voice.

Desperate to reach her, Kaelen pleaded with the Aurora, the great weaver of the night sky. Moved by the silent mountain’s immense devotion, the Aurora spun threads of shimmering starlight and wove them deep into Kaelen’s rocky canyons, turning the dull granite into resonant crystal. 

The next morning, when Zephyr cried out into the void, "I am alone!" the starlight within the stone caught her words. Gently, the mountain replied, "Alone... alone."

Realizing she was finally heard, Zephyr wept with joy. She remained with Kaelen forever, filling his peaks with endless music. Today, the echoes we hear in deep valleys are the remnants of Kaelen’s enduring love, answering the wind.
myths.Agent_2:
Myth: Before the oceans settled, Kaelen, the eldest of the mountain giants, kept watch over the frozen north. He was a creature of profound silence, his body composed of unyielding basalt. His only joy was Zephyra, a vibrant spirit of the winter winds, who painted the sky with auroras and sang melodies of frost and light. Yet, Zephyra grew despairing, believing her songs were lost to the endless, uncaring tundra.

Kaelen longed to reassure her, to tell her that her music was the pulse of his stony heart. Unable to speak, Kaelen struck his own chest with a massive fist, cracking open his granite shell to reveal a vast, hollow cavern lined with shimmering amethyst. 

Startled by the thunderous sound, Zephyra flew down and called into the breach, "Why do you break?" 

The amethyst cavern, resonating with the very soul of the giant, caught her words and gently sang them back: "Why do you break?"

Realizing Kaelen had made himself a vessel for her voice, Zephyra wept with joy. She never abandoned the frozen peaks. Today, when you call into a deep cavern, it is the heartbeat of a mountain returning your song.
